sentences.text: Yeah, right in my bottom-- lower-back.
You know, I was surprised Mark was in Plaszow.
Yeah, only a short time.
Because he was-- you know he was in Krakow?
Could have sent him to Mielec, maybe.
Because he left Krakow before they went to the ghetto.
And--
Is that right?
Goerlitz?
The Poecking, that other camp was Poecking.
Like I said, from the beginning, we arrived 400 men, April the 1st.
We were liberated May the 2nd.
Three days before liberation-- before our liberation, we seen they were setting explosives around the camp, because the American forces, they were approaching.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were going to blow up the camp.
They didn't want-- they didn't want to leave any traces.
So, there were four of us.
We were very close friends with each other.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We decided we're going to escape.
We were going to be killed, anyhow, so what do we have to do is we take a chance.
So about three days before liberation, we escape.
There were no electric fences, wires, just the regular wires, barbed wires.
So at night, we get out of the barracks.
In this-- in that camp, the guards, they were Hungarians, Hungarian soldiers.
They fought with the Germans on the German side.
Right.
Don't forget.
So they were guarding us.
They didn't care so much.
So we escaped, four of us.
My friend lives in Antwerp, Belgium.
One lives in New York.
I think he passed away, already.
And his brother, which he lives in-- in Tel Aviv, on Herzliya Street.
His name was, before the war, Adam Naparstek.
Today, his name is Adam Naor.
Naor, in Hebrew, means naparstek.
Naparstek, it's a-- what do you call it?
It's a thing, so where women use it for sewing.
Needle?
A thimble.
Yeah, thimble, yeah.
So that's his name.
So we escape.
We ran.
We ran.
We ran at night till we reach a German farm.
We ran to the barn.
We hid in the straw-- in the hay.
The farmer didn't know.
Nobody knew who I-- because we knew Americans are coming.
They should be at any time.
They were already in the city.
Our camp was about five miles out of the city.
Oh, yes.
We had a uniform, sure.
And not only that, to recognize us, our heads were shaved.
In the middle of the head, they used to shave us with a blade, like a streak.
We used-- we used to call this 'lausestrasse.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because we were so sick, all of us, already, with typhus.
Because they didn't feed us, at all, in the camp.
They had nothing to eat, themselves, German.
I seen the soldier.
You see the potato shelf.
And the officers, you see the potatoes, because we used to peel for them.
And they didn't feed us at all.
So wherever anything was growing on the-- in the camp, we clean up.
There was no-- there was not even grass growing.
We couldn't eat it.
We-- there is nothing.
It was so-- we were so sick.
And everybody had diarrhea and was sick.
So we escape.
We saw, in the morning, the farmer comes around to the barn and gets the hay for the cattle.
And one of our friends, this fellow in New York, got hit, with the fork, on the shoulder.
And he start to scream.
And the farmer got scared, too.
He know-- he know who we were, because he see the uniform we had and our heads, everything.
And we start to beg him.
We start to cry, please, don't kill us.
Don't take us back to camp.
He could have taken us to camp.
Or he could have-- I don't know what to say, really.
And he was smart.
He knew the American were coming.
So he didn't want to take any chances to send us back to camp.
In case we survive, what would happen to him?
And second, maybe he was a good guy?
But thanks to him, I'm alive today, maybe.
I don't know.
Do you know his name?
Huh?
I don't remember his name, anymore.
I used to correspond with him, I'll be honest with you.
But my friend, in Israel, he keeps in touch with him.
He took his wife down there to see him and his family.
I was going to go.
My wife don't want to go.
If I go to Germany, I'll see him.
So what happened?
And he hid us like that.
He said to us, shh, be quiet, be quiet.
Don't cry, don't cry.
I know-- I'm not going to hurt you, he said to us.
I'll be right back.
We had no choice.
We were in his hands.
So he went home.
He came back a few minutes later, with his wife.
They brought us milk and water and the bread.
And they start to feed us.
They keep us like this for three days, almost.
And one day, May the 2nd, he comes around with the soldiers.
[SIGHS] We were-- I never seen, in my life, American soldier, a soldier, green uniform, with the guns, and you know?
And there were so many different kind of nationality in the German army, too.
There were Hungarian.
There were the Serb-- the Yugoslavian, the-- the Serbian, you know?
And there were all kinds.
And he comes down with the soldiers.
And we cry.
We beg him, please, don't kill us, don't kill us.
And the American soldier said, we're American.
They were a couple of Jewish guys.
There were four soldiers.
There were a couple of Jewish fellows.
And they started to talk Jewish.
One guy, his name was Benny Schulmann, and the other one was Messing.
Yeah, Emmanuel Messing, I have the pictures of them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they started to talk to us, Jewish, this and that.
And this fellow, the Benny Schulmann-- I was so sick, already, I couldn't walk.
I had the typhus.
He took his shirt off from his back.
He wrapped me up in this-- in his shirt.
He carried me, like a little baby, in his hands.
And all of us, we couldn't walk.
The Americans, finally, we're liberated.
They didn't have no time to blow up the camp.
They escaped.
They left the camp intact.
They came in, in this, and liberated the camp.
They built for us an Army hospital in the tents.
I spent three months in the army-- the United States Army Hospital.
I had typhus.
May the 2nd, 1945.
83rd Division.
So after liberation-- like I said, we arrived 400 men.
After liberation, there were 98 of us left alive.
All died.
They weren't killed.
They were dying from starvation.
And right by our camp was a big ditch, a quarry.
It was a quarry.
So we used to take the bodies, dump them in the quarry, cover it up with the stones.
Every time somebody died, we'd just take them down and dump them and cover it up with the stones.
So after the liberation, you know, I come back to myself.
I was in a hospital.
The American government, they called themself a general government.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we used to work with them.
They used us.
They were hunting for the Nazis.
I could smell a Nazi or SS trooper miles away.
You live with them, you know how they walk, you know how they bend, how to-- how they act.
So we used to help-- we used to hunt them.
So one time, me and my friend from Antwerp-- a couple American soldier came in from CIC.
They took us, because we could-- you know, there-- it was with a Polish fellow, American soldier, but he was Polish.
He was a sergeant.
And he came with a list to the-- he wanted a list from the mayor of the little town, from the village.
The village-- the name of-- the name of the village was Kirchham, where we used to build the airfield-- Kirchham.
So we go with him.
And he goes to mayor.
He said, to the men, I should give him the list from the party members, the Nazis or the SS troopers, who they-- they-- they were hunting for them.
So the man said, we don't-- we don't have anybody.
We're all farmers here.
We don't have.
He said, if you're not going to give it to me, I'm going to kill you.
He takes his side arm.
He-- I-- I must have this.
So he gave him the names.
Meantime, this-- this mayor, he was a carpenter.
He had a carpenter shop.
And I was-- I used to work, during the camp, in the carpenter work.
And I was interested.
So we're walking around.
He was building caskets.
He was building, he was selling caskets.
Somebody dies.
And I see caskets staying down there.
And I was walking around with my friend, open up and close them, open up.
So I-- one time, I open up the casket, I see a guy's legs.
And I thought, well, he was dead, maybe.
And I look.
And I recognized him.
He was-- he was one of our-- he was a German fellow.
He was one of our kapos.
He was hiding down there.
But he was German.
He wasn't Polish or a different nation.
He was a German fellow.
And he was such a son of a bitch.
I'm sorry to say this word.
You know, I should watch myself.
But I can't help myself.
He was so mean to us.
And I recognized him.
And we grab him.
Me and my friend, we got him out of the casket.
And this American soldier comes out, what's going on here?
So we tell him who he was.
He said, take him outside.
There was a big apple tree outside in this yard. 'Put him against the tree.' And he start to question him, the American soldier.
He don't want to talk, you know?
He said-- he takes the gun, gives me the pistol-- 'kill him.' So I took the gun.
I-- I never hold a gun in my hand.
I start to shake.
I would tremble like-- I couldn't pull the trigger.
I just couldn't.
As much as I hate him, you know, I should have killed him.
I couldn't.
I just couldn't do it.
He got mad at me, started to call me all kinds of names in Polish.
He took the gun, gave to my friend in Belgium.
He couldn't do it, either.
He said, 'what kind of people are you?' 'I'll show you what to do.' 'I'm a Pollock,' he said to me-- to us.
He killed-- they killed a lot of my family.
And they-- he just, bang, bang, bang.
He shot him right there.
I couldn't do that.
This was a wartime.
He killed him, right there, in this backyard.
I couldn't do it.
I should have done, maybe, but I couldn't do it.
So that's how it was, after we-- right after liberation.
Then they want us to live in a camp.
I didn't want to go back to camp.
They made it-- Because I have a picture of the camp.
I don't want to go to my camp.
But right next to our camp, across the road, there was a big camp, army camp, German army camp, air force camp, nice barracks, beautiful landscaping, everything.
So what they did, they made it a DP camp.
People would start to come over, from different small camps, and from Poland, from different-- you know, from the Russian side.
And they made a DP camp.
Which one was it?
Poecking.
But I never forget, after our liberation-- I never knew about General Patton or something like that.
He came around in his-- in his jeep to our camp.
You know, I knew he was a general, because I could see the stars on his helmet.
Because in Europe, Poland, also, the general had the stars, you know?
But I don't know about Patton.
Who was a Patton to me?
Nobody, as far as I was concerned, you know?
Later on, I find out, you know, you see these two guns here.
The-- there were the brown, high boots he wore.
And they said he was the General Patton.
Later on, I find out it was General Patton.
But I-- these days, I didn't know who we was.
So he came down.
He was the one who liberated us and our camp.
So I don't want to go back to camp.
And my friend, either, from Belgium, and the others.
So I had a friend of mine, who was with us, too.
He wasn't sick.
He moved to a little town, they called Simbach, Simbach am Inn.
It's not too far from Passau, Regensburg, down, right on the border of Braunau, where Hitler was born.
It go in the river.
It goes through.
Across the river was Braunau.
So we were in the barracks.
And he comes around, my friend from Sweden, on a horse.
It was only about a short trip from Simbach to Poecking, you know, maybe around, maybe, 10, 12 miles.
And he came on his horse.
He rode on his horse, white horse, in his uniform from camp.
And he put the name, 'Israel,' on his hat.
Sig-- his name was Siegfried Grauman.
He said to us, you come live with me.
I give you the best apartment you want.
He made himself mayor in this little town, self-proclaimed mayor, if they like it or not.
I be here tomorrow with a wagon and horses.
And I'll pick you up.
There was no cars, then.
So next day, he came around, with the horses and the wagon, pick me up and my friend from Belgium and a couple others.
And we moved to this little town, because I don't want to live in camp.
I had enough of the camp.
I want to-- I live-- I want to live with people, with civilian people.
So we walk around the town.
First, we slept, all five of us in one bed.
It was such a huge bed he had.
So we slept together.
It was nice to sleep in a bed for a change, you know?
[LAUGHS]
And the next day we were looking for apartment.
So I-- so I said to him, I wanted the best apartment.
The biggest Nazi lived in town.
Sure.
So we went in.
And his name was Gfroerer.
His son there was a colonel in the SS troops.
He was in Russia.
He never came back.
But his wife was home, her mother, and her father.
They had a big store, grocery store.
So I knew food was no problem.
So I went upstairs.
I said to her, I want you to clean my-- clean up my room here.
I'm coming over tonight.
I'm going to sleep here.
She said, 'what?' I said, 'you heard me.' You clean up this room for me.
I want a clean bed, clean sheets, clean bed, everything.
She clean up.
In these days, you know, we had the upper hand.
So I lived down there in this town.
I had different kind of apartments, a few others.
You know, I don't like this one, after all, so I had another one.
So we switch it up.
There were only six of us live in this little town.
Most of them, they live in the-- the displaced person camp.
I don't want to live in displaced person camps.
After liberation, I weighed 75 pounds.
I should have said that.
And I was 18 1/2 years old. 75 pounds, I weighed.
Same age.
This one, in Israel, he's a little bit older.
He was about a couple years older.
Huh?
Yes.
My name from this-- this fellow from Belgium, his name is Samuel Sool.
We called him [NON-ENGLISH].
We all had nicknames.
And oh, not only that, when we were in camp, in Auschwitz, to recognize each other, you know, we had a-- a certain word.
Because they plant a lot of spies between us, too, the Germans, I want you to know.
If you didn't know a person, you talk to him.
So first you ask him, amchu.
If he didn't know what was it mean, you know he wasn't kosher.
If it was a Jewish fellow, amchu.
So you were free to talk about.
You know, because you were afraid to talk.
We didn't know who you were talking to.
You know, this was our password, amchu.
So my name is Samuel Sool.
And this other fellow was, again, Naparstek, Adam Naor.
I seen him in Israel when I was there for the--
--ninth--
Romek, they always call me Romek.
Always Romek, yeah.
In this town, I live from 1945 to 1949, four years.
To occupy myself, what I did?
I did what everybody else did, which I should have did different things.
I would be much better off today, I think.
I had no chance-- I didn't want to go to school.
I'll be honest with you.
Which I should have gone, maybe.
I didn't want to give them satisfaction, go to work someplace, to learn some kind of trade, which I had the opportunity in Germany.
But I thought, well, why should I work for them?
I made a mistake.
If I would work for one day, I could have collect Social Security today, as far as that.
So we did nothing, just run around, do black market thing, do a lot of business all day long.
Then the evening, we play cards.
We travel, traveled all over Europe, [LAUGHS] like everybody else.
I wouldn't say, everybody else.
Whoever was smart enough, girls and-- and drinking, stuff like that, you know.
We didn't live normal, don't forget.
We weren't normal people.
I was so hungry for-- for civilization.
You know, after all, we spent all these years in a camp.
And you were always-- you didn't live normal life.
Always sounded like, put you in a cave, then let you out.
You know what to do, where to turn.
I was searching.
I traveled, one town to another in Germany, looking for somebody.
Went to Feldafing, which I found friends of mine from my hometown.
They were in Feldafing camp in Germany.
I traveled to different countries.
I was Hungary.
I went to-- to Italy.
Travel all over, you know, just to pass time, and did black marketing.
Then I finally settled down.
I had a cousin in Germany, my-- my father's cousin.
Then remember the trial for the Nazis, Hermann Goering, Rudolf Hess, all the big Nazis?
And my father's cousin, he was one of the judges.
His name was Reitler.
And his wife was a-- a secretary.
She used to take the notes in the trial.
So they live in Nuremberg.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Meantime, when I was living in Germany, I wanted to find out-- I couldn't find anybody.
After liberation, the American government brought us the books, like a telephone books.
The joint distribution about all-- all the Jewish people names United States.
And I knew I had an uncle in the United States.
I didn't know his address.
I know where he-- I knew he lived in New York.
Because the last correspond we got from him was 1940.
I have a letter from him, too, as a matter of fact, [NON-ENGLISH].
My mother wrote to him.
And my uncle kept the letter.
I have with the open up and the seal, the swastika, as far like that, you know?
So I knew I had an uncle.
And I want to find out how to get in touch with him.
So I went to the book-- went to Muenchen.
On the Siberstrasse was a Jewish Gemeinde down there, a Jewish congregation, on the Siberstrasse, in Germany, in Munich.
So I looked at the book.
And I look, look for Mendler, Mendler.
And find-- and I see his name, Emil Mendler.
I knew he live in New York.
And I had another uncle of mine, too, my mother's cousin, Mendler, also.
So I-- whoever I seen, I wrote them a letter.
Took the address, and I wrote to them.
Meantime, my uncle, from New York, he moved to Uniontown.
He made money from Uniontown.
He moved to Latrobe.
But my other uncle, my mother's cousin-- I call him uncle-- he received a letter from me, too.
I wrote to everybody.
Even to my father's uncle, too, Reibeisen, too.
He live in New York, I wrote to them, too.
Because I took the addresses.
Whoever I could find, I wrote letters.
I told them who I was.
I survived.
I'm the only one so far.
I don't know, I'm searching for the rest of the family, whatever, you know?
And I needed help from them.
I'd like to-- I didn't need financially help, but I needed help mentally and everything.
So anything they know about, maybe somebody register, already, in the family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So meantime, my uncle from-- from New York moved to Latrobe, opened up the business in Latrobe.
And I start to correspond with him.
In the second letter I got from him, he didn't ask no question.
He sent me affidavits, right away.
You come to United States.
Before you left Europe, can you tell me if you tracked down any of your relatives?
And what happened to them?
Can you tell us, you know, in the near--
I didn't get-- well, yes.
I tried.
You know, I was searching.
I didn't want to go to Poland, because that's what happened to my friend.
I told you, previously, what happened to them.
They--
--killed them all in Poland.
So I didn't want to go back, again.
But some friends of mine went down there-- back.
And they find out what happened to our loved ones.
So I knew.
I started to learn what happened to my mom, my sister, and everybody else.
So I sit down.
And I start-- it took me a go-- a good while.
All in all, after liberation, I lost 72 people in my entire family.
And I'm the only one survivor.
Here are some names of my immediate family, for instance, which come-- when there come holidays-- so I say Yizkor-- during the Yizkor services.
So everybody's looking at me-- so many names.
There's Avram Mendler.
There's a Henry Mendler.
You know, we're named after our loved ones.

Samuel Mendler, Jacob Mendler, Morris Mendler, Willie Mendler, Emil Mendler, Max Mendler, Regina Mendler, Helena Mendler, Anda Mendler, Frydka Mendler, Gustav Korn, and Mala Korn, Anna Korn, Leon Korn, my brother, Gershon Reibeisen, my grandfather, Elayza Reibeisen, my father, Mundek Reibeisen, my uncle, Nisim-- Nusim Reibeisen, Yankel Reibeisen, Faiga Reibeisen, Balcia Reibeisen, Ruzia Reibeisen, Dora Reibeisen, Hania Reibeisen, Henia Reibeisen, Hermina Reibeisen.
This was my immediate family.
But all together, my cousins and my uncles and my distant cousins, second cousins, 72 people, I figure out.
I don't know how many more.
You know?
Well, you were very young.
You probably didn't even know half the family--
No.
But I knew exact-- what I-- what I knew of them.
But I'm sure there must be more, which I didn't even know.
I didn't-- Our family, we were very close to each other.
It was always a family affair.
There was no secret.
I knew exactly where my father kept the money and so forth, on everything, you know, because we were in business.
For instance, now they're coming out with the money in-- in-- Switzerland
--Switzerland.
I knew I had an uncle, Neugroschel, on the outside of Vienna.
And he kept all the money down there.
But of course, he is a relation, too, you know, left over?
And you know, this cousin, while he was there, one of the judges in the Nuremberg trial, he's from the Neugroschel family.
I'm sure, if he's alive, he's still going to apply, maybe.
You know, I hope so, anyhow.
So I have my affidavit in hand.
And I wait.
I register.
That's why I couldn't go to Poland with my friends.
Because I had to go to the Polish embassy to sign the application coming to United States.
My uncle wrote me, right away.
I want you to come to the United States.
I don't have children.
I want you to come over here.
I want you to live with me.
You're going to have a job.
You're going to have living quarters, everything.
I want you to come over here.
Of course, I didn't want to part with my friends.
My friends, they were all going to Palestine.
There was no Israel, then.
My-- my best friend, he lives in Antwerp.
I said to my uncle, I wrote him, if he's not going to come with me, I'm not coming.
So my uncle had to send him affidavit, also.
But I tell you the story, what happened.
OK.
